The ES StatefulSet also uses `elastic-password` secret. So after I updated that secret to the new password, the ES pods will eventually pick up the new password when they restart. But currently, they're using the old password since the pods haven't restarted yet.

The problem is: Kubernetes doesn't automatically restart pods when a secret they're referencing changes. The pods need to be manually restarted.

Actually, wait. Secrets can be mounted as volumes (in which case they're auto-updated within ~1 minute) or as environment variables (via `envFrom` or `env` with `secretKeyRef`). 

When secrets are passed as environment variables, the pods must be restarted to pick up the new values. So right now the ES pods still have the old password in their `ELASTIC_PASSWORD` env var, which is why their readiness probes are failing—they're trying to authenticate with the old credentials but Elasticsearch now requires the new password.

Since the pods aren't ready, the `es-http` service has no endpoints and auth-checker can't reach Elasticsearch. I need to restart the ES StatefulSet to force the pods to reload the secret, though I should be mindful about cluster stability during the rolling restart since all pods might restart together depending on the pod management policy. With StatefulSet rolling updates, the `updateStrategy` determines the order—it starts from the highest ordinal pod and works down, regardless of the `podManagementPolicy`. So I'll go ahead with the restart and monitor the cluster to ensure the Elasticsearch pods come back up with the new password environment variable, which should then allow the auth-checker to pass its readiness probe.
